text: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are currently the most common non-viral delivery method used in the field of gene delivery. Even though it is well established that the properties such as size, charge and surface modifications have crucial impacts on their functionality, how these physicochemical characteristics affect the biological behaviors of LNPs post-injection is not fully understood. In our study, a set of NPs with defined size, lipid charge and PEGylated lipid structure were formulated to deliver luciferase mRNA (mLuc) for the first time, to investigate how different NPs characteristics affect gene expression and bio-distribution upon intramuscular injection. We found that smaller, un-charged LNP exhibited higher gene expression in muscle tissues, even though particle size and charge had less effect on NP bio-distribution in vivo. Meanwhile, shorter acyl chain in the PEGylated lipid contributed to better NP functionalities such as improved internalization, endosomal release at the muscle cells, and modulating the mechanisms of different internalizations pathways accordingly. Therefore, Our work here will allow us to clearly understand the detailed requirements for each composition to generate effective intramuscular delivered mRNA for either vaccine or gene therapy applications using LNP.
explanation: Plausible, domain-specific content with minor casual phrasing and small grammar issues; these traits are more consistent with human-authored text than typical AI-generated prose.
